speaker: interviewer
text: On the 23rd of November, 1995. You were telling me about going into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a-- a lot has happened before that too, but I don't think we can talk about it. Well, finally, I got through, and I met someone that knew someone, and that someone had some ways to get to the-- where they were assigning the rooms. And I got a tiny little room where I was able-- I had to run back to Cracow, get my brother and my sister. My sister was-- yeah, she came back to help me to pack, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- this man that was transferring our things, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was bringing the things into this room. The room was the size, if you take from here to there, half of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so, he-- my father's library, not complete because when they saw, when the Nazis, the SS people, saw, at one stage, that he has a, a book printed in the-- Gutenberg printer. Of course, they had taken a few of those antiques. But the rest I try to save for him. So that was the primary thing, and then I-- sheets and, like, linen and something to wear and pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the man brought it to the ghetto. And I needed a wardrobe-- that was a problem-- which we had. Or was it in there? I can't remember. It was a white one. No, we wouldn't have it in the home. It must have been there. So this three-door or four-door wardrobe, it took the whole wall, has taken the books, not the German books. I didn't care about the German. Just my father's, what was precious to him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to make home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister came back, but I said to my sister, go and stay with Father so that he wouldn't be alone, until-- if you feel that somebody recognizes a strange face, come back to the ghetto. That was by train-- I don't remember-- one and a half hours. But I said, remember to take off the Star of David. You had to remember it. And be aware that if you do, you are facing a bullet. But we lost our mother. We have to save Dad. But she was quite bright, and she knew what to do. Well, at one stage, we even managed to bring my father to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was Pesach or something-- to the ghetto. I got a passage from, from him-- for him. So legally, we brought him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, the Germans took over our properties and the income from the properties. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at least we were together, without our mother, so it was most crying then anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like that. They were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were confiscating [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever was good, valuable from Jewish homes, from the District of Cracow. It was brought into the ghetto and that there were a few buildings in which they used-- they stored it. Then they were catching from the street and told us to sort out everything and wash everything because it was shipped to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The hatred towards them was so big that the people starting urinating in it after it was washed and then wipe it and said, let them eat from-- they are spitting. You know, as terrible as, as the times were, it made me sick looking what they were doing, because I think at that time I thought we are turning into animals just like they are. I couldn't bring myself to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you should have seen the most beautiful Rosenthals and whatnot, but these are just things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So well, and then, of course, when this finished-- or it didn't finish yet-- I was on the way to there, they would grab me, take me to the city. And we had to clear the streets from the snow. And on one occasion, they grabbed myself and my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hadn't eaten, probably for three days. We just drunk water. And it was cold water from the tap. There was nothing to put-- to heat up the place. We used up all the papers that were around, like, you know, the newspapers. It was-- in the ghetto, we had our own newspaper, but nothing much to read about. The post office, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember where I was. Oh, yes. They were grabbing us, catching us from the street, to clean the streets off the snow or the snow off the streets. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember we were so hungry and so cold that our legs were stiff and our hands were stiff. I couldn't keep the shovel, because my fingers were stiff. And my sister was completely stiff.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a place, the most luxurious restaurant, always was, but, of course, Jews like my father, our family, never went there because it wasn't a Jewish restaurant. It's still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hawelka. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this smell from this restaurant and this empty stomach, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't describe it. It's, it's-- I, I really-- even in my mother language, I wouldn't find words to describe the pain. And I had a few, some, few cents in the pocket. And I said to my sister, you know, Eda, I will go inside, and I'll see whether they'll give us a bowl of soup for that money. I, I didn't have any idea of what costs what. And she said to me, but it's not kosher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to her, look, it says in our religion that if you have to save your life, you're allowed to eat not kosher. And she, she was frightened. But I went inside. I asked for the person who owns it. And I said, I-- look, you can see from-- with my Star of David that I'm Jewish, but my sister and I, we haven't eaten for three days. I was never begging in my life, and I'm not begging you now. I've got a few cents. I don't know whether it buys a spoon of soup or a bowl of soup, but you will save a life, and you may one day be proud of it. And he said, look. And there were Germans watching us. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took my-- I went out to my sister, and I said, you come close to the door there, but be inconspicuous so that the Wehrmacht soldiers wouldn't see you. They were not-- the Wehrmacht wasn't bad. And wait for me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came in. And he gave me a big bowl of soup. Didn't want the money I gave him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister said, I'm afraid. It's not kosher. If you swallow it, you may choke. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, well then, I will choke. And she said, I can't be left alone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I said, do you want to be the first to choke? I had no doubts that we won't choke. So I said, I'll tell you what. Let's start together from that bowl. And we're eating this bowl of soup. I went back to that man. We had to eat very fast because they would notice that we are not there. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to see the man, and I said, if I ever survive and if I ever come back here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will look you up. Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Hawelka two years ago, but I didn't know who I had to ask for. I didn't know his name. And he was an elderly man, so he wouldn't-- anyhow. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Well, we didn't choke.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then occasionally my sister was going back to my father's, but every Friday I was trying to make a little challah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No fire. So between my neighbors and myself, we try to break chairs and-- you know, to make a fire and make a challah. How did your sister go in and out the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your sister go in and out the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out the-- no, she was, she was taken from the ghetto to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to, to your father. To go to your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she could go? She was allowed out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She had to take off the Star of David, pretend she's not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was at the gate of the ghetto? Who was guarding the gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a German police and a Polish policeman. But we had the passes because my uncle, who owned a factory before the war-- factories and businesses were taken over by so-called German [NON-ENGLISH]. So my uncle made a deal with a German [NON-ENGLISH]. He says you can have the factory. You can have everything you want. I'll give you some money. I don't care what you do with the factory, whether you run it, you burn it, or whatever, but I want from you passes from the ghetto-- this was this uncle that I have shown to you, the girl, my cousin-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for my family passes. And let us that they work in that factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for himself, but then he had, I think, Argentinian passport, and stayed outside the ghetto. Because if you had a foreign passport, not a Polish one, even though you were Jewish, you were allowed to live outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you and your brother and sister have those passes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So that's how we could get out. And how I kept getting some money is I-- each time I took some linen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whatever I had, curtains that I took off from-- not from all the rooms, but from two rooms-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so each time I went to Cracow, I sold something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I sold it-- it may seem very peculiar-- I suddenly became enterprising. I bought some sausage, and I bought butter, but that was already later. It was summer. It was very hot. And I put the butter underneath here and kept it with the belt and the sausage. Not too much of it, but I thought I'll sell one and, and keep one for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, you won't believe me. Until I got to, to Cracow and came back to the ghetto-- I came home. I was swimming in butter, but there was no butter to eat or any butter to sell. It was just running. And the sausage, you wouldn't want to smell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever part of any Aktions in the, in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Aktion. The Aktion. We were all in the Aktion. But each time, we had to get a different permission to stay. One was a blue [INAUDIBLE], and one was such a [INAUDIBLE] and another [INAUDIBLE]. But all in all, I went through a few of those. But don't forget. I was young. I was a young-- you can say I was quite attractive. I wouldn't say I was a, a beauty. I wasn't Princes Di. But something they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke German when I want--

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at the time frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Are you thinking about the camera?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.
